content.text: Helicone is an open-source LLM observability platform that works as an HTTP proxy rather than an SDK you integrate into your code. You point your LLM client's base URL at Helicone's gateway, add an API key header, and every request is transparently forwarded to the actual provider while Helicone captures latency, token counts, cost, and request/response bodies. The result is that you get observability data with a one-line configuration change and zero instrumentation code in your agent logic.

Because Helicone sits in the request path, it can also actively modify requests: return cached responses for identical prompts, enforce per-user rate limits, and inject prompt versions from its template store. Self-hosting is supported through Docker Compose with a PostgreSQL + ClickHouse backend, the same architecture as the cloud version.

attrs.alt: Helicone proxy model: agent code sends to helicone.ai which logs and forwards to the LLM provider, versus traditional SDK approach requiring code instrumentation throughout

content.text: For Anthropic, set the base_url to Helicone's Anthropic gateway and add the Helicone-Auth header as a default header. Every subsequent client.messages.create() call is logged automatically — no other changes needed.

pip install anthropic
export HELICONE_API_KEY="sk-helicone-..."   # from helicone.ai/settings
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."

import os
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic(
    base_url="https://anthropic.helicone.ai",
    default_headers={
        "Helicone-Auth": f"Bearer {os.environ['HELICONE_API_KEY']}",
    },
)

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain Helicone in one sentence."}],
)
print(response.content[0].text)
# Helicone dashboard at helicone.ai/requests now shows this call:
# model, latency, prompt/completion tokens, cost estimate, request body
The OpenAI gateway uses a different base URL but the same pattern: one URL, one header.

Custom properties are arbitrary key-value pairs you attach to individual requests via Helicone-Property-* headers. They appear as filterable columns in the dashboard. Use them to tag requests by user, session, feature, environment, or any other dimension you care about.

def call_support_agent(user_id: str, session_id: str, message: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": message}],
        extra_headers={
            # Any Helicone-Property-* header is stored as a custom property
            "Helicone-Property-UserId": user_id,
            "Helicone-Property-SessionId": session_id,
            "Helicone-Property-Feature": "support-agent",
            "Helicone-Property-Env": "production",
        },
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# In the dashboard you can now filter:
#   Feature = support-agent  AND  Env = production
# and see cost / latency / error rate for exactly that slice

The Helicone-Session-Id header groups all requests from one conversation into a session view. Helicone-Session-Path lets you label the agent's position in the flow (for example, the step within a multi-agent pipeline). All turns with the same session ID appear together in the Sessions tab with a combined cost and latency rollup.

Helicone exposes a feedback endpoint that lets you attach a thumbs-up / thumbs-down score to any logged request. You get the request ID from the Helicone-Id response header and POST a rating back. The ratings appear in the dashboard alongside the original request so you can filter for low-rated calls and inspect what went wrong.

Helicone can cache LLM responses at the proxy layer. When Helicone-Cache-Enabled is true, repeated identical requests (same model, same messages) return the cached response in milliseconds without hitting the provider. This is useful for development (no cost for repeated test queries) and for production flows where many users ask the same FAQ-style questions.

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=256,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is the capital of France?"}],
    extra_headers={
        "Helicone-Cache-Enabled": "true",
        # Optional: set cache TTL in seconds (default 7 days)
        "Helicone-Cache-Bucket-Max-Size": "10",  # max bucket variants
    },
)
# First call: forwarded to Anthropic, response cached
# Subsequent identical calls: returned from cache, latency < 50ms
print(response.content[0].text)

Helicone enforces rate limits at the proxy level using the Helicone-RateLimit-Policy header. The policy value is a quota string: N requests per time-window per unit (user ID, session, or globally). When the limit is exceeded, Helicone returns a 429 without forwarding the request to the provider.

Helicone's prompt management feature stores prompt templates with version history. You define a template in the dashboard or via API, then reference it by ID in requests using the Helicone-Prompt-Id header. The request is logged with the prompt version, so you can see which version of a prompt produced which outputs and compare quality across versions.

import re

def call_with_versioned_prompt(
    template_id: str,
    variables: dict,
    template_text: str,
) -> str:
    # Fill the template locally (Helicone records the template_id + variables)
    prompt = template_text
    for key, value in variables.items():
        prompt = prompt.replace(f"{{{{{key}}}}}", str(value))

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        extra_headers={
            "Helicone-Prompt-Id": template_id,
        },
    )
    return response.content[0].text

reply = call_with_versioned_prompt(
    template_id="support-v3",
    variables={"customer_name": "Alice", "issue": "billing"},
    template_text="Help {{customer_name}} with their {{issue}} question.",
)
print(reply)
# In the dashboard: filter by Prompt ID 'support-v3' to see all uses,
# compare against 'support-v2' for quality regression analysis
Querying Data via the Helicone API

Helicone's REST API lets you pull request logs programmatically for offline analysis, building custom dashboards, or computing aggregates the web UI does not expose. You filter by time range, custom properties, model, or status.

To run Helicone on your own infrastructure, clone the repository and start it with Docker Compose. The default compose file starts the proxy (Worker), the API server, the web app, PostgreSQL (request metadata), and ClickHouse (analytics). After startup, point your clients at http://localhost:8787 instead of helicone.ai.

git clone https://github.com/Helicone/helicone
cd helicone
cp .env.example .env   # edit OPENAI_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, etc.
docker compose up -d

# Worker (proxy)    → http://localhost:8787
# Web UI            → http://localhost:3000
# API server        → http://localhost:8585
Yes — tool calls, retrievals, custom spans

Helicone's Experiments feature lets you run two prompt variants against the same set of inputs and compare outputs side by side. You define a dataset of production requests, create a variant prompt, and Helicone replays the inputs through your agent code calling the two variants. Results are collected in the dashboard with per-variant cost, latency, and — if you add scores — quality metrics.

import os
import anthropic
import uuid

# Tag each request with an experiment ID and variant so you can
# slice the dashboard by experiment to compare A vs B.

VARIANT_A_PROMPT = "Answer the following question concisely: {question}"
VARIANT_B_PROMPT = "You are a helpful assistant. Answer this question in a friendly tone: {question}"

client = anthropic.Anthropic(
    base_url="https://anthropic.helicone.ai",
    default_headers={"Helicone-Auth": f"Bearer {os.environ['HELICONE_API_KEY']}"},
)

def call_variant(question: str, variant: str, prompt_template: str) -> str:
    experiment_id = "exp-tone-comparison-v1"
    filled = prompt_template.format(question=question)
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": filled}],
        extra_headers={
            "Helicone-Property-ExperimentId": experiment_id,
            "Helicone-Property-Variant": variant,
            # Stable session per question so A and B results are linked
            "Helicone-Session-Id": f"{experiment_id}-{uuid.uuid5(uuid.NAMESPACE_DNS, question)}",
        },
    )
    return response.content[0].text

questions = [
    "What is a vector database?",
    "How does RAG work?",
]
for q in questions:
    a_out = call_variant(q, "A-concise", VARIANT_A_PROMPT)
    b_out = call_variant(q, "B-friendly", VARIANT_B_PROMPT)
    print(f"A: {a_out[:80]}")
    print(f"B: {b_out[:80]}")
    print()

# In the dashboard: filter Property.ExperimentId = exp-tone-comparison-v1
# then break down by Property.Variant to compare cost and latency between A and B

Helicone adds a hop between your application and the LLM provider. In testing, the added latency is typically 20–80 ms. For latency-critical paths, measure the overhead in your environment before relying on Helicone in the hot path, or use self-hosted Helicone co-located with your service to minimize the added round-trip.

By default, Helicone logs full request and response bodies including user messages. If your application handles PII or confidential information, either self-host Helicone so data never leaves your network, or enable Helicone's omit-inputs / omit-outputs options (Helicone-Omit-User: true and Helicone-Omit-Response: true headers) to log metadata only.

If the Helicone cloud gateway is unavailable, your agent cannot reach the LLM provider either, because the proxy sits in the request path. Helicone publishes uptime at status.helicone.ai. For production systems, consider whether a fallback base URL (pointing directly at the provider) is needed, or use self-hosting for stronger availability guarantees.

Helicone normalizes custom property names by stripping the Helicone-Property- prefix and using the remainder as the key. The casing of the key is preserved. Helicone-Property-UserId and Helicone-Property-userid create two separate properties in the dashboard. Standardize your casing across all request sites.

Helicone's cache key is the full request body including the model and messages. If you change any part of the prompt or add a new system message, the cache miss is immediate — you get a fresh provider call, not the stale cached answer. This is correct behavior but can be surprising during iterative prompt development if you expect the old cached answer.
No tool-call or retrieval span visibility.

Because Helicone is a proxy that sees only HTTP calls to the LLM provider, it cannot observe tool execution, vector database queries, or any custom agent logic that runs between LLM calls. Those spans require an SDK-based tool (Langfuse, Phoenix, OTel). Helicone is best paired with custom-property tagging for coarse-grained slicing, not with deep per-span instrumentation.

Helicone is a proxy-based observability platform: set base_url to the appropriate Helicone gateway and add a Helicone-Auth header, and every LLM call is logged with latency, token counts, cost, and request/response bodies — no decorators, no SDK integration, no changes to business logic. Custom properties (Helicone-Property-* headers) tag requests for filtering, and Helicone-Session-Id groups multi-turn conversations. Built-in caching (Helicone-Cache-Enabled) and rate limiting (Helicone-RateLimit-Policy) are controlled through the same header mechanism. The feedback API lets you attach user ratings to individual requests. Helicone works best when you want low-friction observability for LLM calls and need caching or rate limiting out of the box; for deep agent traces with nested tool call spans and LLM-judge evaluations, a full SDK-based tool like Langfuse or Phoenix gives more granular control.
